The Invisible Generation

Youth mortality and school attendance as mirrors of global inequality

Author

Rohith Ramesh

Published

April 14, 2026

Introduction

Across the developing world, the countries where children cannot access school are the same countries where young people aged 15–24 die at the highest rates. This is not coincidence — it is the geometry of inequality.

Using UNICEF data on youth mortality and primary school attendance, combined with World Bank economic indicators, this report tells the story of a generation left behind. The data spans 1990 to 2022 and covers countries across every continent.

Key findings:

  • Sub-Saharan Africa carries a disproportionate burden of both youth mortality and school exclusion
  • Males consistently face higher mortality rates than females across all regions
  • Countries with lower GDP per capita have significantly lower school attendance rates
  • Despite global progress, the gap between rich and poor nations remains stark

Data Loading

Code 
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
from plotnine import *

ind1 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")
ind2 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2.csv")
meta = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv")

print("Indicator 1 (Youth Mortality):", ind1.shape)
print("Indicator 2 (School Attendance):", ind2.shape)
print("Metadata (World Bank):", meta.shape)
Indicator 1 (Youth Mortality): (20298, 14)
Indicator 2 (School Attendance): (367, 14)
Metadata (World Bank): (13715, 15)

Data Transformations

Code 
# Filter mortality for Total sex only, rename columns
mortality = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .rename({"obs_value": "mortality_rate", "time_period": "year"})
)

# Filter attendance for Total sex only
attendance = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .rename({"obs_value": "attendance_rate", "time_period": "year"})
)

# Clean metadata - rename GDP column
meta2 = meta.rename({
    "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)": "gdp_per_capita",
    "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_expectancy"
})

# Join attendance with metadata on country code and year
attendance_gdp = (
    attendance
    .join(
        meta2.select(["alpha_3_code", "year", "gdp_per_capita"]),
        on=["alpha_3_code", "year"],
        how="left"
    )
    .drop_nulls(subset=["gdp_per_capita"])
    .to_pandas()
)

# Get peak mortality per country for world map
mortality_latest = (
    mortality
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("mortality_rate").max().alias("max_mortality"))
    .sort("max_mortality", descending=True)
    .to_pandas()
)

# Get worst 20 countries for school attendance
worst_20 = (
    attendance
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("attendance_rate").mean().alias("avg_attendance"))
    .sort("avg_attendance")
    .head(20)
    .to_pandas()
)

# Get trend data by gender for time series
trend = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex").is_in(["Male", "Female"]))
    .rename({"time_period": "year"})
    .group_by(["year", "sex"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_mortality"))
    .sort("year")
    .to_pandas()
)

print("Top 10 highest mortality countries:")
print(mortality_latest.head(10).to_string(index=False))
Top 10 highest mortality countries:
                 country  max_mortality
                  Rwanda     862.878421
Central African Republic     440.474490
             South Sudan     288.972939
                 Burundi     134.755558
                 Eritrea     133.792077
                 Somalia     127.523457
  Bosnia and Herzegovina     115.166607
                 Liberia     111.132671
                  Angola      96.869368
                   Congo      91.929254

Chart 1 — World Map of Youth Mortality

Where do young people die the most? Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia stand out clearly as hotspots of youth mortality.

Chart 2 — Worst 20 Countries for School Attendance

South Sudan has a primary school attendance rate of under 25% — the lowest in the world. All 20 of the worst-performing countries are in Sub-Saharan Africa or South Asia.

Code 
(
    ggplot(worst_20, aes(
        x="avg_attendance",
        y="reorder(country, avg_attendance)",
        fill="avg_attendance"
    ))
    + geom_col()
    + scale_fill_gradient(low=CORAL, high=GOLD)
    + labs(
        title="The 20 Countries with Lowest School Attendance",
        subtitle="Average primary school attendance rate (%)",
        x="Average Attendance (%)",
        y="",
        caption="Source: UNICEF"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=BG),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=PAN),
        text=element_text(color=TEXT),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        legend_position="none"
    )
)

Chart 3 — School Attendance vs GDP per Capita

The relationship between wealth and education is clear — every increase in GDP per capita correlates with higher school attendance. Countries in the bottom-left corner (low GDP, low attendance) are the same ones shown in red on the map above.

Code 
(
    ggplot(attendance_gdp, aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="attendance_rate"))
    + geom_point(color=CORAL, alpha=0.5, size=2)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color=BLUE)
    + labs(
        title="Wealthier Countries Keep More Children in School",
        subtitle="Primary school attendance rate vs GDP per capita",
        x="GDP per Capita (USD, constant 2015)",
        y="School Attendance (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF & World Bank"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=BG),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=PAN),
        text=element_text(color=TEXT),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13),
        panel_grid=element_line(color="#1e2a40")
    )
)

Chart 4 — Youth Mortality Trend Over Time

Both male and female youth mortality have declined since 1990 — a genuine global achievement. However, males consistently die at higher rates than females, and this gender gap has barely closed in 30 years.

Code 
(
    ggplot(trend, aes(x="year", y="avg_mortality", color="sex"))
    + geom_line(size=1.5)
    + geom_point(size=2)
    + scale_color_manual(
        values={"Male": CORAL, "Female": BLUE},
        name=""
    )
    + labs(
        title="Three Decades of Progress — But the Gap Remains",
        subtitle="Global average youth mortality rate (ages 15-24) by gender",
        x="Year",
        y="Avg. Deaths per 1,000 Youths",
        caption="Source: UNICEF"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_background=element_rect(fill=BG),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill=PAN),
        text=element_text(color=TEXT),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13),
        panel_grid=element_line(color="#1e2a40"),
        legend_background=element_rect(fill=BG)
    )
)

Conclusion

The data tells a consistent and urgent story. Youth mortality and school exclusion are not random; they cluster in the same countries, the same regions, driven by the same underlying force: poverty.

  • Sub-Saharan Africa faces the highest mortality and lowest attendance of any region
  • Wealthier nations keep children in school and alive longer
  • The gender gap in mortality has persisted for over 30 years despite global progress
  • Every percentage point increase in school attendance correlates with lower mortality rates

The children in these statistics are not abstractions. They are real young people aged 15 to 24 — the age of ambition, of possibility, of life just beginning. The countries that fail to keep them in school are the same countries where they die before their time.

“The Invisible Generation are not invisible because they don’t exist. They are invisible because the world has not yet chosen to see them.”
